narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 84% chance that you would not pass based on your academic profile. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with stronger indicators of bar success to maintain high pass rates. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, where your position in the bottom tenth stood out as the strongest signal pushing toward a failure prediction—far below the middle-upper range typical for those who passed. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, which also pulled the prediction toward failure compared to the top-third averages seen in successful bar takers. Your LSAT score of 30 further reinforced this risk, as it fell well short of the 37-plus average among those who passed, making it the third key factor tipping the scales. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was close to the passing average of 3.24 and your lower-middle family income bracket aligns with a smaller but present group of successful students, these did not outweigh the top concerns. Being full-time is common among passers, so it didn't shift the outcome much either. In essence, your early and later undergraduate performance deciles, combined with the LSAT, painted a picture riskier than the profiles of accepted applicants, who typically rank higher across these metrics. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward 37 or above, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying once these stand out more favorably. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by targeting these areas, and admissions teams often review improved profiles sympathetically. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
